title: The Vasculitis Syndromes
authors: Carol A. Langford, Anthony S. Fauci

items.subtopic: The Vasculitis Syndromes | DEFINITION
items.title: Understanding Vasculitis: Inflammation and Ischemia
items.content: Vasculitis is characterized by inflammation of blood vessels, leading to compromised vessel lumen and subsequent ischemia in the tissues supplied. It can manifest as a primary disease or secondary component of another condition, affecting single or multiple organ systems.
keyPoints: Inflammation leads to compromised vessel lumen
Associated with tissue ischemia
Can be primary or secondary manifestation
May affect one or multiple organs

items.subtopic: The Vasculitis Syndromes | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Immunopathogenic Mechanisms in Vasculitis
items.content: Vasculitic syndromes are often mediated by immunopathogenic mechanisms responding to antigenic stimuli. Factors like genetic predisposition, environmental exposures, and immune response regulation play roles. Immune complexes, ANCA, and T lymphocyte responses are hypothesized mechanisms.
keyPoints: Immunopathogenic mechanisms are common
Antigenic stimuli trigger vasculitis in some individuals
Genetic predisposition and environmental factors contribute
Immune complex formation, ANCA, and T cell responses are key hypotheses
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · The Vasculitis Syndromes | DEFINITION | CLASSIFICATION | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | PATHOGENIC IMMUNE-COMPLEX FORMATION

items.subtopic: The Vasculitis Syndromes | PATHOGENIC IMMUNE-COMPLEX FORMATION
items.title: Role of Immune Complexes in Vasculitis Pathogenesis
items.content: Immune complex deposition was once the primary pathogenic mechanism for vasculitis, but its role is not clearly established. Circulating immune complexes do not always lead to vasculitis, and many active cases lack detectable complexes. Hepatitis B antigen has been identified in some systemic vasculitis resembling polyarteritis nodosa.
keyPoints: Immune complex deposition was historically central
Causal role of immune complexes is unclear
Active vasculitis may not show circulating/deposited complexes
Hepatitis B antigen linked to some systemic vasculitis

items.subtopic: ANTINEUTROPHIL CYTOPLASMIC ANTIBODIES
items.title: ANCA and Vasculitis Syndromes: Diagnostic Markers and Clinical Implications
items.content: ANCA are antibodies targeting proteins in neutrophil granules, associated with ANCA-associated vasculitis including granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis. cANCA primarily targets proteinase-3, found in over 90% of active GPA cases, while pANCA targets myeloperoxidase, associated with MPA and other conditions.
keyPoints: cANCA (cytoplasmic ANCA) targets proteinase-3; >90% of active granulomatosis with polyangiitis patients have detectable antibodies to proteinase-3.
pANCA (perinuclear ANCA) primarily targets myeloperoxidase, associated with microscopic polyangiitis and other conditions.
ANCA presence does not always correlate with disease activity; high titers can persist in remission.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · ANTINEUTROPHIL CYTOPLASMIC ANTIBODIES

items.subtopic: ANTINEUTROPHIL CYTOPLASMIC ANTIBODIES
items.title: Pathogenic Role of ANCA in Vasculitis: Mechanisms and Controversies
items.content: The pathogenesis of vasculitis syndromes involving ANCA is complex. Activated neutrophils may degranulate and produce reactive oxygen species, causing tissue damage. However, the direct pathogenic role of ANCA is debated as patients can have active disease without ANCA or maintain high titers during remission.
keyPoints: ANCA-activated neutrophils cause tissue damage via degranulation and reactive oxygen species production.
Clinical observations suggest ANCA may not play a primary pathogenic role; active disease can occur without ANCA, and high titers can persist in remission.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · ANTINEUTROPHIL CYTOPLASMIC ANTIBODIES

items.subtopic: PATHOGENIC T LYMPHOCYTE RESPONSES AND GRANULOMA FORMATION | General Principles of Diagnosis
items.title: Role of Pathogenic T Lymphocytes in Granulomatous Vasculitis
items.content: Pathogenic T lymphocyte responses contribute to granulomatous vasculitis through endothelial cell activation and cytokine secretion. Vascular endothelial cells express HLA class II molecules after activation by IFN-γ, allowing interaction with CD4+ T lymphocytes similar to macrophages. These cells secrete IL-1, activating T lymphocytes and promoting immunologic processes within blood vessels. Additionally, IL-1 and TNF-α induce ELAM-1 and VCAM-1, enhancing leukocyte adhesion.
keyPoints: Endothelial cells express HLA class II molecules following activation by IFN-γ.
IL-1 secretion activates T lymphocytes and initiates immunologic processes in blood vessels.
IL-1 and TNF-α induce ELAM-1 and VCAM-1, enhancing leukocyte adhesion.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · PATHOGENIC T LYMPHOCYTE RESPONSES AND GRANULOMA FORMATION | General Principles of Diagnosis

items.subtopic: PATHOGENIC T LYMPHOCYTE RESPONSES AND GRANULOMA FORMATION | General Principles of Diagnosis
items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Vasculitis
items.content: Consider vasculitis in patients with unexplained systemic illness, especially when presenting with palpable purpura, pulmonary infiltrates and microscopic hematuria, chronic sinusitis, mononeuritis multiplex, unexplained ischemic events, or glomerulonephritis with multisystem disease. Exclude nonvasculitic diseases, particularly infections, before proceeding. Diagnosis is confirmed via biopsy of involved tissue; avoid 'blind' biopsies. Arteriograms are indicated for suspected polyarteritis nodosa, Takayasu arteritis, or primary CNS vasculitis.
keyPoints: Consider vasculitis in unexplained systemic illness with specific clinical features.
Exclude nonvasculitic diseases and infections before confirming diagnosis.
Definitive diagnosis is usually made via biopsy of involved tissue.
Arteriograms are indicated for suspected polyarteritis nodosa, Takayasu arteritis, or primary CNS vasculitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · PATHOGENIC T LYMPHOCYTE RESPONSES AND GRANULOMA FORMATION | General Principles of Diagnosis

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)
items.title: Immediate Use of Glucocorticoids in Severe Vasculitis
items.content: In vasculitic diseases with established high morbidity and mortality, such as granulomatosis with polyangiitis, immediate initiation of glucocorticoids and/or other immunosuppressive agents is crucial to prevent irreversible organ dysfunction.
keyPoints: Immediate use of glucocorticoids in severe systemic vasculitides like granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
Glucocorticoids should be tapered and discontinued when possible after improvement.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)
items.title: Monitoring and Preventing Toxicity in Vasculitis Treatment
items.content: Regular monitoring of complete blood counts is essential to prevent cytopenias when using immunosuppressive agents like cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, azathioprine, and mycophenolate mofetil. Maintaining WBC count >3000/μL and neutrophil count >1500/μL reduces infection risk.
keyPoints: Monitor complete blood counts every 1–2 weeks during cyclophosphamide treatment.
Maintain WBC count >3000/μL and neutrophil count >1500/μL to reduce life-threatening infections.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)
items.title: Infection Prophylaxis in Vasculitis Patients on Immunosuppressive Therapy
items.content: Patients treated with glucocorticoids and other immunosuppressants, especially those with granulomatosis with polyangiitis or microscopic polyangiitis, should receive prophylactic therapy against Pneumocystis jirovecii. TMP-SMX is recommended during induction treatment.
keyPoints: Prophylaxis for Pneumocystis jirovecii in patients on glucocorticoids and other immunosuppressants.
TMP-SMX or alternative prophylactic medication should be used during induction therapy.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 2) | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Individualized Treatment Approach in Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.content: While treatment guidelines provide a framework, each patient with granulomatosis with polyangiitis requires individual decision-making. The goal is to maximize therapeutic efficacy while minimizing toxic side effects by incorporating patient-specific goals.
keyPoints: Treatment should be evidence-based and tailored to the individual.
Guidelines serve as a framework but are not absolute.
Patient-specific goals are crucial for effective treatment.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 2) | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 2) | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis and Immune Response in Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.content: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis is characterized by necrotizing vasculitis and granuloma formation, often involving the respiratory tract and kidneys. The disease shows a $ T_{H}1 $-type cytokine imbalance, with increased IFN-$ γ $ and TNF-$ α $ production, suggesting an aberrant cell-mediated immune response.
keyPoints: Histopathologic features include necrotizing vasculitis and granuloma formation.
$ T_{H}1 $-type cytokine imbalance is evident with increased IFN-$ γ $ and TNF-$ α $.
ANCA autoantibodies may play a role in pathogenesis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 2) | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis: Upper Airway and Pulmonary Involvement
items.content: Upper airway involvement is common in granulomatosis with polyangiitis, occurring in 95% of patients. It presents with severe symptoms like paranasal sinus pain and purulent or bloody nasal discharge. Subglottic stenosis can lead to severe airway obstruction in ~16%. Pulmonary involvement affects 85–90% of patients, presenting as cough, hemoptysis, dyspnea, chest discomfort, or asymptomatic cases.
keyPoints: Upper airway involvement occurs in 95% of patients.
Symptoms include paranasal sinus pain and purulent/bloody nasal discharge.
Subglottic stenosis occurs in ~16%, potentially causing severe airway obstruction.
Pulmonary involvement affects 85–90% of patients, with symptoms like cough, hemoptysis, dyspnea, or asymptomatic cases.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis: Renal and Laboratory Findings
items.content: Renal disease is predominant in granulomatosis with polyangiitis, affecting 77% of patients. It can lead to rapidly progressive renal failure if untreated. Characteristic laboratory findings include elevated ESR/CRP, anemia, leukocytosis, mild hypergammaglobulinemia, and thrombocytosis. Approximately 90% of active cases have positive antiproteinase-3 ANCA.
keyPoints: Renal disease affects 77% of patients and can lead to rapidly progressive renal failure if untreated.
Laboratory findings: elevated ESR/CRP, anemia, leukocytosis, mild hypergammaglobulinemia, thrombocytosis.
Approximately 90% of active cases have positive antiproteinase-3 ANCA.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis of Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis via Tissue Biopsy
items.content: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis can be diagnosed by identifying necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis in tissue biopsy, particularly from pulmonary or upper airway tissues. Pulmonary biopsies have the highest yield for revealing granulomatous vasculitis.
keyPoints: Necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis on tissue biopsy confirms diagnosis
Pulmonary tissue offers the highest diagnostic yield
Upper airway tissue shows acute/chronic inflammation and may reveal granulomas or vasculitis
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Role of ANCA in Diagnosing Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.content: The utility of ANCA varies; it has a 90% predictive value in patients with active glomerulonephritis but only 30-60% when sinus/lung disease is present without renal involvement. Tissue biopsy is often necessary to rule out infections or malignancies that mimic the presentation.
keyPoints: Positive ANCA predictive value: 90% with active glomerulonephritis
Predictive value decreases to 30-60% in sinus/lung disease without renal involvement
Tissue biopsy needed when clinical features are inconsistent or ANCA is absent
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Differentiating Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis from Other Midline Destructive Diseases
items.content: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis must be differentiated from other midline destructive diseases like upper airway neoplasms and cocaine-induced lesions. These conditions can mimic the disease but have distinct histological features or clinical presentations.
keyPoints: Differentiate from upper airway neoplasms, e.g., NK/T-cell lymphoma
Cocaine-induced lesions may show ANCA targeting human neutrophil elastase
Levamisole adulteration in cocaine can mimic vasculitis with granulocytopenia
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.title: Impact of Cyclophosphamide on Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis Outcomes
items.content: The introduction of cyclophosphamide treatment significantly improved the prognosis for patients with granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Prior to this, the disease was universally fatal within months. With cyclophosphamide, marked improvement is seen in over 90% of patients, complete remission in 75%, and a 5-year survival rate exceeding 80%.
keyPoints: Marked improvement in >90% of patients with cyclophosphamide treatment
Complete remission achieved in 75% of patients
5-year patient survival exceeds 80%
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.title: Management and Relapse Considerations in Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.content: Despite successful induction of remission, 50–70% of patients experience relapses. Objective evidence is necessary to confirm relapse, as ANCA levels alone are not reliable indicators due to their persistence post-remission and a low correlation with immediate disease activity. Treatment involves two phases: induction and maintenance, tailored by disease severity and patient factors.
keyPoints: 50–70% of remissions later associated with relapses
ANCA levels alone are not reliable for determining relapse
Only 43% of patients relapsed within 1 year of increased ANCA levels
Treatment involves induction to achieve remission and maintenance to prevent relapse
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: REMISSION INDUCTION OF SEVERE DISEASE
items.title: Reduced-Dose Glucocorticoids in Remission Induction
items.content: A reduced-dose glucocorticoid regimen is noninferior to a standard-dose regimen for inducing remission of severe active disease, with a lower rate of serious infection.
keyPoints: Historical glucocorticoid regimen: prednisone 1 mg/kg per day for the first month, followed by tapering
Reduced-dose regimen is noninferior to standard-dose
Lower rate of serious infections with reduced-dose regimen
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · REMISSION INDUCTION OF SEVERE DISEASE

items.subtopic: REMISSION INDUCTION OF SEVERE DISEASE
items.title: Avacopan as an Alternative in Remission Induction
items.content: Avacopan, a C5a receptor inhibitor, can be used with cyclophosphamide or rituximab to induce remission, allowing for rapid glucocorticoid withdrawal while maintaining similar adverse event rates.
keyPoints: Avacopan investigated as an alternative to glucocorticoids
Higher sustained remission at 52 weeks compared to prednisone
Similar rate of serious adverse events between avacopan and prednisone groups
Markedly less glucocorticoid exposure in the avacopan group
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · REMISSION INDUCTION OF SEVERE DISEASE

items.subtopic: CYCLOPHOSPHAMIDE INDUCTION FOR SEVERE DISEASE
items.title: Induction with Cyclophosphamide: Efficacy and Monitoring
items.content: Daily cyclophosphamide combined with glucocorticoids is effective for inducing remission in severe disease. It's administered at 2 mg/kg per day orally, but dosage adjustments are necessary for patients with renal insufficiency due to its renal elimination. A comparison trial showed that IV cyclophosphamide (15 mg/kg every 2 weeks initially) had a similar remission rate but higher relapse frequency compared to daily oral administration.
keyPoints: Cyclophosphamide is given at 2 mg/kg per day orally for induction.
Dosage reduction needed in renal insufficiency due to renal elimination.
IV cyclophosphamide (15 mg/kg every 2 weeks initially) showed similar remission but higher relapse than daily oral regimen.
Leukopenia more common with daily cyclophosphamide, likely due to less frequent blood count monitoring after the first month.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CYCLOPHOSPHAMIDE INDUCTION FOR SEVERE DISEASE | RITUXIMAB INDUCTION FOR SEVERE DISEASE

items.subtopic: RITUXIMAB INDUCTION FOR SEVERE DISEASE
items.title: Rituximab as an Alternative for Severe Disease Induction
items.content: Rituximab, administered at 375 mg/m² once a week for 4 weeks along with glucocorticoids, is effective for inducing remission in granulomatosis with polyangiitis and microscopic polyangiitis. It has been shown to be as effective as cyclophosphamide for newly diagnosed severe disease and superior in relapsing cases, without the bladder toxicity or infertility risks associated with cyclophosphamide.
keyPoints: Rituximab dosed at 375 mg/m² once a week for 4 weeks is effective for remission induction.
Comparable efficacy to cyclophosphamide in newly diagnosed severe disease.
Superiority over cyclophosphamide in relapsing disease cases.
No bladder toxicity or infertility risks, similar adverse event rates as cyclophosphamide.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CYCLOPHOSPHAMIDE INDUCTION FOR SEVERE DISEASE | RITUXIMAB INDUCTION FOR SEVERE DISEASE

items.subtopic: REMISSION MAINTENANCE OF SEVERE DISEASE
items.title: Rituximab vs. Azathioprine for Remission Maintenance
items.content: When transitioning from cyclophosphamide to a remission maintenance therapy, rituximab is preferred over azathioprine due to its lower relapse rate. Rituximab is administered at 500 mg initially and then every 6 months.
keyPoints: Rituximab shows a lower rate of relapse compared to azathioprine (2 mg/kg per day).
Rituximab dosing: 500 mg for two doses followed by 500 mg every 6 months.
Methotrexate and azathioprine have similar rates of toxicity and relapse when used for remission maintenance.
Maintenance therapy duration is usually a minimum of 2 years, but individualized based on risk factors like organ damage or history of relapse.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · REMISSION MAINTENANCE OF SEVERE DISEASE

items.subtopic: REMISSION MAINTENANCE OF SEVERE DISEASE
items.title: Methotrexate Dosage and Maintenance Strategy
items.content: For remission maintenance, methotrexate is started at 15 mg/week orally or subcutaneously, with increments of 2.5 mg every 2 weeks up to a maximum of 20-25 mg/week.
keyPoints: Methotrexate starting dose: 15 mg/week, increased by 2.5 mg every 2 weeks.
Maximum methotrexate dosage for maintenance is 20–25 mg/week.
Similar relapse and toxicity rates observed with methotrexate compared to azathioprine in randomized trials.
Consideration of longer-term therapy if there's significant organ damage or history of relapse.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · REMISSION MAINTENANCE OF SEVERE DISEASE

items.subtopic: REMISSION INDUCTION AND MAINTENANCE OF NONSEVERE DISEASE | OTHER BIOLOGIC AGENTS | ORGAN-SPECIFIC TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.title: Induction and Maintenance of Remission in Nonsevere Vasculitis
items.content: For nonsevere vasculitis, methotrexate or mycophenolate mofetil with glucocorticoids can be used to induce and maintain remission. Rituximab is an option but requires careful risk-benefit analysis. Cyclophosphamide is generally not recommended for nonsevere granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
keyPoints: Methotrexate or mycophenolate mofetil with glucocorticoids can be used to induce and maintain remission in nonsevere disease.
Rituximab may be considered, weighing risks and benefits.
Cyclophosphamide is rarely justified for nonsevere granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · REMISSION INDUCTION AND MAINTENANCE OF NONSEVERE DISEASE | OTHER BIOLOGIC AGENTS | ORGAN-SPECIFIC TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: REMISSION INDUCTION AND MAINTENANCE OF NONSEVERE DISEASE | OTHER BIOLOGIC AGENTS | ORGAN-SPECIFIC TREATMENT | DEFINITION
items.title: Use of Biologic Agents in Nonsevere Vasculitis
items.content: Etanercept and belimumab do not sustain remission or reduce relapse risk when used adjunctively in granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Abatacept did not show efficacy in reducing relapse, disease worsening, or failure to achieve remission.
keyPoints: Etanercept should not be used as it does not sustain remission in granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
Belimumab showed no added benefit in reducing the risk of relapse when used with azathioprine.
Abatacept did not reduce the risk of relapse, disease worsening, or failure to achieve remission.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · REMISSION INDUCTION AND MAINTENANCE OF NONSEVERE DISEASE | OTHER BIOLOGIC AGENTS | ORGAN-SPECIFIC TREATMENT | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Microscopic Polyangiitis
items.title: Understanding the Pathogenesis and Clinical Features of Microscopic Polyangiitis
items.content: Microscopic polyangiitis is an ANCA-associated vasculitis affecting small vessels, with a predilection for capillaries, venules, and small- to medium-sized arteries. Unlike other vasculitides, it lacks immunoglobulin deposition in vascular lesions, indicating that immune-complex formation does not contribute to its pathogenesis. Clinically, it presents similarly to granulomatosis with polyangiitis but without upper airway disease or pulmonary nodules.
keyPoints: Incidence: 3–5/100,000
Mean age of onset: ~57 years
Slightly more common in males
Paucity of immunoglobulin deposition suggests no role for immune-complex formation
ANCA association present in 75% of patients, predominantly antimyeloperoxidase antibodies
Glomerulonephritis occurs in at least 79% of patients
Alveolar hemorrhage occurs in 12% of patients
5-year survival rate with treatment: 74%
Disease relapse observed in at least 34% of patients
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Microscopic Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Microscopic Polyangiitis
items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Treatment Considerations for Microscopic Polyangiitis
items.content: Diagnosis of microscopic polyangiitis relies on histologic evidence of vasculitis or pauc-immune glomerulonephritis, alongside clinical features consistent with multisystem disease. ANCA testing is crucial, as 75% of patients test positive, primarily for antimyeloperoxidase antibodies. Treatment mirrors that of granulomatosis with polyangiitis, focusing on managing systemic involvement and preventing relapses.
keyPoints: Diagnosis based on histologic evidence or pauc-immune glomerulonephritis
ANCA testing essential; 75% positive for antimyeloperoxidase antibodies
5-year survival rate with treatment: 74%
Disease relapse in at least 34% of patients, requiring tailored management
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Microscopic Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Recognizing Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA)
items.content: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, formerly known as Churg-Strauss syndrome, is characterized by asthma, eosinophilia, and vasculitis affecting multiple organ systems. It is an uncommon disease with a distinct clinical presentation.
keyPoints: Annual incidence: 1–3 per million
Mean age of onset: 48 years
Female-to-male ratio: 1.2:1
Characteristic eosinophilia >1000 cells/μL in >80% of patients
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Laboratory Findings in EGPA
items.content: EGPA presents with multisystem involvement, predominantly affecting the lungs. Key clinical features include asthmatic attacks, mononeuritis multiplex, allergic rhinitis, sinusitis, and heart disease. Laboratory findings often show significant eosinophilia and inflammation markers.
keyPoints: Pulmonary infiltrates and asthma are predominant
Mononeuritis multiplex occurs in up to 72% of patients
Allergic rhinitis/sinusitis: up to 61%
Heart disease (myocarditis, pericarditis, etc.): ~14%
Skin lesions (~51%) include purpura and nodules
Circulating ANCA present in ~48% of patients
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Challenges for EGPA
items.content: Diagnosis of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA) requires evidence of asthma, peripheral blood eosinophilia, and clinical features consistent with vasculitis. Histologic confirmation can be challenging as pathognomonic features may not occur simultaneously.
keyPoints: Requires evidence of asthma, peripheral blood eosinophilia, and vasculitis
Histologic confirmation is often challenging due to non-simultaneous occurrence of pathognomonic features
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS | Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.title: Prognostic Importance and Initial Evaluation in EGPA
items.content: Untreated EGPA has a poor prognosis, with a 5-year survival rate of 25%. Treatment improves outcomes significantly. Echocardiography is crucial for all newly diagnosed patients due to myocardial involvement being the most frequent cause of death.
keyPoints: 5-year survival rate of untreated EGPA: 25%
78-month actuarial survival rate with treatment: 72%
Myocardial involvement causes 39% of patient mortality
Echocardiography is recommended for all newly diagnosed patients
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS | Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Characteristics and Diagnostic Features of Polyarteritis Nodosa
items.content: Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN) is a multisystem necrotizing vasculitis affecting small- to medium-sized muscular arteries, typically involving renal and visceral arteries but not pulmonary ones. Key diagnostic features include segmental lesions at arterial bifurcations, absence of glomerulonephritis or significant eosinophilia, and the presence of aneurysms up to 1 cm.
keyPoints: PAN affects small- and medium-sized muscular arteries, notably renal and visceral but not pulmonary arteries.
Lesions are segmental, often at bifurcations, with potential for aneurysmal dilations up to 1 cm.
Absence of glomerulonephritis, granuloma, significant eosinophilia, or venule involvement distinguishes PAN from other vasculitides like microscopic polyangiitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis and Associated Conditions of Polyarteritis Nodosa
items.content: The pathogenesis of PAN involves necrotizing inflammation leading to fibrinoid necrosis, thrombosis, and ischemic tissue damage. It is associated with hepatitis B via immune complex deposition in vessel walls. Other associations include hepatitis C, hairy cell leukemia, and DADA2 deficiency, which presents differently and responds to TNF inhibitors.
keyPoints: Pathogenesis involves necrotizing inflammation, fibrinoid necrosis, thrombosis, and ischemia.
PAN is associated with hepatitis B through immune complex deposition in vessel walls.
Differential associations include hepatitis C, hairy cell leukemia, and DADA2 deficiency, which requires consideration of TNF inhibitors.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Polyarteritis Nodosa: Identifying Key Clinical Features and Diagnostic Approach
items.content: Polyarteritis nodosa presents with nonspecific symptoms such as fever, weight loss, malaise, weakness, headache, abdominal pain, and myalgias. Renal involvement often manifests as hypertension, renal insufficiency, or hemorrhage due to microaneurysms. Laboratory findings typically include elevated leukocyte count (>75% of patients) with neutrophil predominance, anemia of chronic disease, elevated ESR/CRP, and hypergammaglobulinemia. ANCA is rarely found, and screening for hepatitis B and C is recommended.
keyPoints: Nonspecific symptoms: fever, weight loss, malaise (>50% cases)
Renal involvement: hypertension, renal insufficiency, hemorrhage
Leukocyte count elevated in >75% of patients with neutrophil predominance
Eosinophilia suggests eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis
Anemia of chronic disease, elevated ESR/CRP almost always present
Hypergammaglobulinemia may be seen; screen for hepatitis B and C
ANCA rarely found
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis of Polyarteritis Nodosa: Biopsy and Arteriography
items.content: The diagnosis of polyarteritis nodosa relies on biopsy findings showing vasculitis in involved organs, with high diagnostic yield from symptomatic organ biopsies like skin lesions, testes, or nerve/muscle. In the absence of accessible tissue, arteriographic demonstration of aneurysms or stenotic segments in small- and medium-sized arteries (renal, hepatic, visceral) is sufficient. Catheter-directed dye arteriogram is preferred due to higher resolution compared to MRI/CT arteriograms.
keyPoints: Biopsy shows vasculitis; high yield from symptomatic organ biopsies
Arteriographic demonstration of aneurysms/stenotic segments in small- and medium-sized arteries suffices for diagnosis
Catheter-directed dye arteriogram preferred over MRI/CT arteriograms
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Polyarteritis Nodosa | DEFINITION
items.title: Prognosis and Treatment Impact in Polyarteritis Nodosa
items.content: Untreated polyarteritis nodosa has a very poor prognosis, with significant mortality primarily due to gastrointestinal and cardiovascular complications. The combination of prednisone and cyclophosphamide significantly improves survival rates.
keyPoints: 5-year survival rate without treatment is 10-20%
Death often results from bowel infarcts, perforation, and cardiovascular causes
Prednisone and cyclophosphamide improve survival
Glucocorticoids alone can induce remission in less severe cases
Relapse occurs in 10-20% of patients post-remission
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Polyarteritis Nodosa | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: Polyarteritis Nodosa | DEFINITION
items.title: Management of Hepatitis Virus-associated Vasculitis in Polyarteritis Nodosa
items.content: In polyarteritis nodosa-like vasculitis associated with hepatitis B or C, antiviral therapy is crucial. Immunosuppression is used concurrently for severe cases and withdrawn once the vasculitis is controlled.
keyPoints: Antiviral therapy is essential in hepatitis-associated polyarteritis nodosa
Immunosuppression is given concurrently with antivirals for severe vasculitis
Immunosuppression can be withdrawn after controlling vasculitis
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Polyarteritis Nodosa | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors for Giant Cell Arteritis
items.content: Giant cell arteritis predominantly affects individuals over the age of 50, with a higher incidence in women compared to men. It is rare among black populations. Geographic variations exist, with higher rates reported in Scandinavia and areas of the U.S. with Scandinavian descent, versus lower rates in southern Europe. The annual incidence ranges from 6.9 to 32.8 per 100,000 individuals aged ≥50 years. There is evidence of familial aggregation and genetic associations, particularly with HLA-DR4 and specific alleles at the HLA-DRB1 locus.
keyPoints: Affects >50-year-olds primarily
Higher incidence in women than men
Rare in blacks
Incidence rates: 6.9 to 32.8 per 100,000 aged ≥50 years
Familial aggregation and genetic links with HLA-DR4 and HLA-DRB1 locus
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis of Giant Cell Arteritis: An Antigen-Driven Process
items.content: Giant cell arteritis is characterized by systemic vasculitis affecting medium and large arteries, often undetected. It involves panarteritis with inflammatory infiltrates leading to intimal proliferation and fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina. The disease is antigen-driven, involving activated T lymphocytes, macrophages, and dendritic cells. CD4+ T cells enter through vasa vasorum in the adventitia, activating and orchestrating macrophage differentiation. Key cytokines include IL-2 and IFN-$ γ$, with involvement of $T_{H}1$ and $T_{H}17$ cell lineages in vascular inflammation.
keyPoints: Systemic vasculitis affecting medium and large arteries
Histopathology: panarteritis, intimal proliferation, fragmentation of internal elastic lamina
Antigen-driven disease involving T cells, macrophages, dendritic cells
CD4+ T cell activation in adventitia via vasa vasorum
Cytokines involved: IL-2 and IFN-$γ$; $T_{H}1$ and $T_{H}17$ cells participate
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Recognizing Giant Cell Arteritis: Symptoms and Complications
items.content: Giant cell arteritis (GCA) is characterized by fever, anemia, high ESR/CRP, and headaches in patients aged >50 years. Cranial artery involvement leads to headache, scalp pain, jaw claudication, and potential ischemic optic neuropathy, which can cause sudden blindness if untreated.
keyPoints: GCA presents with fever, anemia, high ESR/CRP, and headaches in patients aged >50 years.
Cranial artery involvement causes headache, scalp pain, jaw claudication, and potential ischemic optic neuropathy.
Ischemic optic neuropathy can lead to sudden blindness if untreated.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Giant Cell Arteritis: Large-Vessel Disease and Systemic Inflammation
items.content: Up to one-third of GCA patients can have large-vessel disease, presenting as subclavian artery stenosis or aortic aneurysms. Systemic inflammation features include malaise, fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, and sweats, with laboratory findings showing elevated ESR/CRP, normochromic/hypochromic anemia, and liver function abnormalities.
keyPoints: Up to one-third of GCA patients can have large-vessel disease presenting as subclavian artery stenosis or aortic aneurysms.
Systemic inflammation features include malaise, fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, and sweats.
Laboratory findings: elevated ESR/CRP, normochromic/hypochromic anemia, liver function abnormalities.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Giant Cell Arteritis
items.content: Giant cell arteritis diagnosis is suggested by clinical presentation in patients >50 years old, including fever, anemia, high ESR/CRP, and symptoms of polymyalgia rheumatica. Confirmatory biopsy of the temporal artery should be 3–5 cm with serial sectioning due to patchy histologic findings. Ultrasonography showing a noncompressible 'halo' sign supports diagnosis. Therapy should not wait for diagnostic studies; glucocorticoid therapy response can further support diagnosis.
keyPoints: Clinical suggestion: fever, anemia, high ESR/CRP in patients >50 years old
Biopsy segment length: 3–5 cm with serial sectioning
Ultrasonography finding: noncompressible 'halo' sign
Therapy initiation: do not delay pending diagnostic studies
Glucocorticoid therapy response: supports diagnosis even if biopsy shows vasculitis after ~14 days
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis of Isolated Polymyalgia Rheumatica
items.content: Isolated polymyalgia rheumatica is diagnosed clinically by stiffness, aching, and pain in the hip and shoulder girdle muscles, increased ESR/CRP, absence of giant cell arteritis features, and response to low-dose prednisone. It can mimic rheumatoid arthritis with peripheral arthritis; however, rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP should be negative. Consider seronegative rheumatoid arthritis if peripheral arthritis worsens. Serum creatine kinase levels are not elevated.
keyPoints: Clinical symptoms: stiffness, aching, pain in hip/shoulder girdle
Laboratory findings: increased ESR/CRP
Therapeutic response: prompt to low-dose prednisone
Differential diagnosis: absence of giant cell arteritis features
Peripheral arthritis mimicry: rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP should be negative
Serum creatine kinase levels: not elevated
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Giant Cell Arteritis
items.title: Why Early Glucocorticoid Therapy is Crucial in Giant Cell Arteritis
items.content: Early initiation of glucocorticoid therapy, specifically prednisone at a dose of 40–60 mg/d for approximately one month followed by gradual tapering, is essential to reduce symptoms and prevent visual loss in giant cell arteritis. Rapid intervention with methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for three days is recommended when ocular signs appear to protect vision.
keyPoints: Prednisone dose: 40–60 mg/d initially for ~1 month
Methylprednisolone: 1000 mg daily for 3 days if ocular symptoms occur
Symptom recurrence during tapering occurs in 60–85% of patients
ESR and/or CRP can guide therapy but minor increases may not indicate exacerbation
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Giant Cell Arteritis

items.subtopic: Giant Cell Arteritis
items.title: When to Consider Tocilizumab for Giant Cell Arteritis
items.content: Tocilizumab, an anti-IL-6 receptor agent, can be considered for patients with giant cell arteritis who experience glucocorticoid toxicity or require a strategy to achieve glucocorticoid-free remission. It has been shown superior to prednisone alone in sustaining remission over a 26-week taper.
keyPoints: Tocilizumab dosage: 162 mg subcutaneously once weekly or biweekly
Superiority demonstrated in achieving glucocorticoid-free remission compared to prednisone alone
Consideration based on comorbidities, glucocorticoid toxicity, and tocilizumab side effects
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Giant Cell Arteritis

items.subtopic: Polymyalgia Rheumatica | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Management of Polymyalgia Rheumatica with Prednisone and Sarilumab
items.content: Patients with polymyalgia rheumatica typically respond well to prednisone at a dose of 10–20 mg/d. Monitoring ESR/CRP is useful during glucocorticoid tapering due to the high likelihood of symptom recurrence. For those resistant or relapsing on ≥7.5 mg/d of prednisone, sarilumab (anti-IL-6 receptor) can be considered as it has shown higher sustained remission rates in a trial when combined with a glucocorticoid taper.
keyPoints: Prednisone dose for polymyalgia rheumatica: 10–20 mg/d
ESR/CRP useful for monitoring during prednisone reduction
Sarilumab effective for glucocorticoid-resistant or relapsing patients on ≥7.5 mg/d of prednisone
Use of sarilumab should be restricted to inadequate response cases
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Polymyalgia Rheumatica | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Polymyalgia Rheumatica | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Takayasu Arteritis: Incidence, Prevalence, and Pathology
items.content: Takayasu arteritis is an inflammatory disease affecting large arteries, particularly the aortic arch and its branches. It has an annual incidence rate of 1.2–2.6 cases per million, predominantly affecting adolescent girls and young women, with higher prevalence in Asia but no racial or geographic restriction.
keyPoints: Incidence: 1.2–2.6 cases per million annually
Most prevalent in adolescent girls and young women
Higher prevalence in Asia, not restricted by race or geography
Pathology involves panarteritis with inflammatory infiltrates, intimal proliferation, fibrosis, scarring, and potential aneurysms
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Polymyalgia Rheumatica | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis
items.title: Recognizing Early Symptoms and Laboratory Findings of Takayasu Arteritis
items.content: Takayasu arteritis presents with generalized symptoms such as malaise, fever, night sweats, arthralgias, anorexia, and weight loss months before vascular involvement. Key laboratory findings include elevated ESR/CRP, mild anemia, and elevated immunoglobulin levels.
keyPoints: Generalized symptoms: malaise, fever, night sweats, arthralgias, anorexia, weight loss
Vascular symptoms may follow generalized symptoms
Hypertension occurs in 32–93% of patients
Characteristic lab findings: elevated ESR/CRP, mild anemia, elevated immunoglobulin levels
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Imaging for Takayasu Arteritis
items.content: Suspect Takayasu arteritis in young women with absent or decreased peripheral pulses, blood pressure discrepancies between arms, or arterial bruits. Diagnosis is confirmed by arteriography showing irregular vessel walls, stenosis, poststenotic dilation, aneurysm formation, occlusion, and increased collateral circulation.
keyPoints: Suspect in young women with absent/decreased peripheral pulses
Blood pressure discrepancy between arms or arterial bruits
Arteriography confirms diagnosis: irregular vessel walls, stenosis, poststenotic dilation, aneurysm formation, occlusion, increased collateral circulation
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis
items.title: Management and Treatment Approaches for Takayasu Arteritis
items.content: Takayasu arteritis is managed with glucocorticoids (prednisone 40–60 mg/d) to alleviate symptoms, though no studies confirm increased survival. Surgical or arterioplastic interventions improve outcomes by reducing stroke risk and correcting hypertension due to renal artery stenosis.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoid therapy: prednisone 40–60 mg/d
No studies confirming increased survival with glucocorticoids
Surgical/arterioplastic interventions improve outcomes
Interventions reduce stroke risk, correct hypertension from renal artery stenosis
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: IgA Vasculitis: Key Clinical Features and Demographics
items.content: IgA vasculitis, also known as Henoch-Schönlein purpura, is a small-vessel vasculitis primarily affecting children aged 4–7 years but can occur in infants and adults. It presents with palpable purpura, arthralgias, gastrointestinal symptoms, and glomerulonephritis.
keyPoints: Most common age group: 4-7 years
Male-to-female ratio: 1.5:1
Seasonal peak incidence in spring
Palpable purpura seen in virtually all pediatric patients
Gastrointestinal involvement in ~70% of pediatric cases
Renal involvement occurs in 10-50% of patients, characterized by mild glomerulonephritis
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Pathogenesis and Laboratory Findings in IgA Vasculitis
items.content: IgA vasculitis is associated with immune-complex deposition, often involving IgA antibodies. Common triggers include infections, drugs, foods, insect bites, and immunizations. Laboratory findings typically show mild leukocytosis, normal platelet count, occasional eosinophilia, normal serum complement components, and elevated IgA levels in about half of the patients.
keyPoints: Pathogenic mechanism: immune-complex deposition
IgA antibodies most commonly involved
Common triggers: infections, drugs, foods, insect bites, immunizations
Laboratory findings: mild leukocytosis, normal platelet count, occasional eosinophilia
Normal serum complement components
Elevated IgA levels in ~50% of patients
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | IgA Vasculitis | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of IgA Vasculitis
items.content: IgA vasculitis is diagnosed primarily through clinical signs and symptoms, with skin biopsy confirming leukocytoclastic vasculitis via immunofluorescence. Renal biopsy is seldom necessary but may offer prognostic insights. Glucocorticoids like prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) are used to alleviate edema, arthralgias, and abdominal discomfort but do not benefit skin or renal disease nor reduce active disease duration or recurrence risk.
keyPoints: Diagnosis based on clinical signs and symptoms
Skin biopsy confirms leukocytoclastic vasculitis with IgA and C3 deposition
Renal biopsy rarely needed, may provide prognostic information
Prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) useful for tissue edema, arthralgias, abdominal discomfort
Glucocorticoids do not benefit skin or renal disease
Disease recurrences in 10–40% of patients
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS | IgA Vasculitis | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | IgA Vasculitis | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis and Clinical Features of Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis
items.content: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, often linked with hepatitis C, involves cold-precipitable immunoglobulins causing systemic vasculitis. Skin biopsies show inflammatory infiltrate and fibrinoid necrosis. Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis accounts for 80% of renal lesions. The pathogenesis is driven by immune complexes formed due to an aberrant response to hepatitis C, leading to inflammation in vessel walls.
keyPoints: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis associated with systemic symptoms and hepatitis C
Skin biopsies reveal inflammatory infiltrate, fibrinoid necrosis, endothelial hyperplasia
Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis in 80% of renal lesions
Immune complexes from hepatitis C trigger vasculitis
5% of chronic hepatitis C patients develop cryoglobulinemic vasculitis
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS | IgA Vasculitis | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis
items.title: Prognostic Implications of Glomerulonephritis in Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis
items.content: Glomerulonephritis is a significant prognostic indicator in cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, with 15% of patients progressing to end-stage renal disease. Additionally, these patients have a high risk (40%) of experiencing fatal cardiovascular disease, infection, or liver failure.
keyPoints: Glomerulonephritis is present in 10–30% of cryoglobulinemic vasculitis cases.
15% progress to end-stage renal disease if glomerulonephritis develops.
40% later experience fatal cardiovascular disease, infection, or liver failure.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis
items.title: First-Line Treatment for Hepatitis C-Associated Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis
items.content: Antiviral therapy is the first-line treatment for hepatitis C-associated cryoglobulinemic vasculitis. Clinical improvement correlates with virologic response, and patients who clear hepatitis C show objective improvements in vasculitis symptoms.
keyPoints: Hepatitis C infection is associated with the majority of cases.
Antiviral therapy leads to clinical improvement dependent on virologic response.
Patients clearing hepatitis C experience reductions in circulating cryoglobulins, IgM, and rheumatoid factor.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis

items.subtopic: SINGLE-ORGAN VASCULITIS | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Understanding Single-Organ Vasculitis: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Single-organ vasculitis is characterized by inflammation confined to the blood vessels of a single organ without systemic involvement. It can be identified during surgeries, such as orchiectomy for testicular masses initially suspected as neoplasms but found to be vasculitis. While some patients may remain asymptomatic and avoid immunosuppressive therapy if the affected organ is removed entirely, others, like those with primary CNS vasculitis, require medical intervention.
keyPoints: Single-organ vasculitis involves arteries or veins in a single organ without systemic features.
Examples include isolated aortitis, testicular vasculitis, and primary CNS vasculitis.
>70% of cutaneous vasculitis cases are associated with systemic vasculitis or secondary causes; 30% occur idiopathically.
In single-organ vasculitis without systemic involvement, close monitoring may suffice if the organ is removed.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · SINGLE-ORGAN VASCULITIS | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: SINGLE-ORGAN VASCULITIS | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathology and Clinical Features of Cutaneous Vasculitis
items.content: Cutaneous vasculitis, the most common type encountered in clinical practice, involves inflammation of dermal blood vessels. It is characterized by leukocytoclasis during acute stages and mononuclear cell predominance in subacute or chronic phases. Postcapillary venules are typically involved, with capillaries and arterioles less frequently affected. Erythrocyte extravasation leads to palpable purpura, a hallmark of this condition.
keyPoints: Cutaneous vasculitis is the most common type encountered in clinical practice.
Leukocytoclasis refers to nuclear debris from neutrophils during acute stages.
Postcapillary venules are primarily involved; capillaries and arterioles less frequently.
Palpable purpura results from erythrocyte extravasation.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · SINGLE-ORGAN VASCULITIS | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis
items.title: Recognizing and Diagnosing Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis
items.content: Idiopathic cutaneous vasculitis is primarily characterized by skin involvement, with palpable purpura being the most common manifestation. Other forms can include macules, papules, vesicles, bullae, subcutaneous nodules, ulcers, and urticaria. Lesions typically appear in lower extremities or sacral areas due to hydrostatic forces. Diagnosis requires biopsy confirmation of vasculitis and ruling out systemic causes.
keyPoints: Palpable purpura is the hallmark skin lesion.
Lesions commonly occur on lower extremities or sacral area.
Mild leukocytosis, eosinophilia, elevated ESR/CRP are characteristic lab findings.
Biopsy confirms diagnosis; search for etiology essential.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis
items.title: Management Strategies for Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis
items.content: Treatment of idiopathic cutaneous vasculitis depends on identifying and removing any antigenic stimuli. If no inciting agent is found, treatment decisions weigh symptom severity against therapy risks. Common treatments include dapsone, colchicine, hydroxychloroquine, NSAIDs, and glucocorticoids like prednisone with tapering. Refractory cases may require immunosuppressants such as methotrexate or azathioprine; cyclophosphamide is rarely used due to toxicity.
keyPoints: Remove antigenic stimulus if identified (e.g., drugs, infections).
Glucocorticoids often used with low-dose prednisone and rapid tapering.
Refractory cases may use methotrexate or azathioprine; cyclophosphamide rarely indicated.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM VASCULITIS | BEHCET'S DISEASE | COGAN'S SYNDROME
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Primary CNS Vasculitis
items.content: Primary CNS vasculitis is a rare condition characterized by inflammation confined to the central nervous system vessels. Diagnosis involves imaging, lumbar puncture, arteriography, and definitive confirmation via brain biopsy. Differential diagnoses include reversible cerebral vasoconstrictive syndrome and other conditions like infections or connective tissue diseases.
keyPoints: Primary CNS vasculitis is characterized by mononuclear cell infiltrates with or without granuloma formation in the CNS vessels.
Patients present with headaches, altered mental function, and focal neurologic defects; systemic symptoms are generally absent.
Diagnosis suggested by MRI, lumbar puncture, arteriography; confirmed by brain biopsy.
Differential diagnosis includes reversible cerebral vasoconstrictive syndrome, infections, atherosclerosis, emboli, connective tissue disease, sarcoidosis, malignancy, and drug-associated causes.
Prognosis is poor for granulomatous primary CNS vasculitis; glucocorticoids with or without cyclophosphamide can induce remission.
Post-remission treatment should switch from cyclophosphamide to azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil due to good CNS penetration.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM VASCULITIS | BEHCET'S DISEASE | COGAN'S SYNDROME

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM VASCULITIS | BEHCET'S DISEASE | COGAN'S SYNDROME
items.title: Characteristics and Treatment of Behçet's Disease
items.content: Behçet's disease is marked by recurrent oral and genital ulcers, iritis, and cutaneous lesions due to leukocytoclastic venulitis. It can affect vessels of any size in various organs.
keyPoints: Behçet's disease involves recurrent episodes of oral and genital ulcers, iritis, and cutaneous lesions.
Pathologic process is a leukocytoclastic venulitis affecting vessels of any size and organ.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM VASCULITIS | BEHCET'S DISEASE | COGAN'S SYNDROME

items.subtopic: KAWASAKI'S DISEASE
items.title: Recognizing and Managing Kawasaki Disease in Children
items.content: Kawasaki disease is a serious febrile illness primarily affecting children under 5 years old. Prompt recognition and treatment are crucial to prevent coronary artery complications.
keyPoints: 80% of cases occur before age 5, with peak incidence at ≤2 years.
Coronary artery aneurysms develop in ~25% of untreated cases.
Case-fatality rate is 0.5–2.8%.
High-dose IV gamma-globulin (2 g/kg) and aspirin are effective treatments when administered early.
Surgical intervention may be necessary for giant coronary artery aneurysms.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · KAWASAKI'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: KAWASAKI'S DISEASE
items.title: Differentiating Kawasaki Disease from MIS-C in the Context of SARS-CoV-2 Infection
items.content: Multisystem inflammatory syndrome (MIS-C) can mimic Kawasaki disease, especially during SARS-CoV-2 outbreaks. Accurate diagnosis is essential for appropriate management.
keyPoints: Kawasaki-like symptoms may occur with SARS-CoV-2 infection as MIS-C.
Atypical features of MIS-C include gastrointestinal symptoms and shock.
Testing for SARS-CoV-2 is recommended in patients presenting with Kawasaki disease-like illness.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · KAWASAKI'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: POLYANGIITIS OVERLAP SYNDROMES | DRUG-INDUCED VASCULITIS | SERUM SICKNESS AND SERUM SICKNESS-LIKE REACTIONS
items.title: Management of Polyangiitis Overlap Syndromes and Drug-Induced Vasculitis
items.content: Polyangiitis overlap syndromes require treatment akin to granulomatosis with polyangiitis, especially when major organ systems are at risk. In drug-induced vasculitis, the severity dictates management; life-threatening cases necessitate glucocorticoids and cyclophosphamide.
keyPoints: Overlap syndromes treated like granulomatosis with polyangiitis if major organs threatened
Drug-induced vasculitis may present as palpable purpura or systemic symptoms
Drugs implicated include allopurinol, thiazides, gold, sulfonamides, phenytoin, penicillin
ANCA-positive drug-induced vasculitis can lead to severe manifestations like glomerulonephritis and pulmonary hemorrhage
Severe cases treated with glucocorticoids and cyclophosphamide
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · POLYANGIITIS OVERLAP SYNDROMES | DRUG-INDUCED VASCULITIS | SERUM SICKNESS AND SERUM SICKNESS-LIKE REACTIONS

items.subtopic: POLYANGIITIS OVERLAP SYNDROMES | DRUG-INDUCED VASCULITIS | SERUM SICKNESS AND SERUM SICKNESS-LIKE REACTIONS
items.title: Characteristics of Serum Sickness and Serum Sickness-Like Reactions
items.content: Serum sickness occurs 7–10 days after primary exposure to a heterologous protein, with symptoms including fever, urticaria, polyarthralgias, and lymphadenopathy. Serum sickness-like reactions occur 2–4 days after secondary exposure to nonprotein drugs like penicillin or sulfa.
keyPoints: Serum sickness occurs 7-10 days post-primary exposure
Symptoms include fever, urticaria, polyarthralgias, lymphadenopathy
Serum sickness-like reactions occur 2-4 days post-secondary exposure to nonprotein drugs
Common drugs involved: penicillin, sulfa
Rare progression from cutaneous venulitis to systemic vasculitis
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · POLYANGIITIS OVERLAP SYNDROMES | DRUG-INDUCED VASCULITIS | SERUM SICKNESS AND SERUM SICKNESS-LIKE REACTIONS

items.subtopic: VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES
items.title: Infections Triggering Vasculitis: Mechanisms and Clinical Implications
items.content: Certain infections can directly trigger vasculitic processes by invading endothelial cells or mimicking primary vasculitis through inflammatory responses. Rickettsias, for example, invade small blood vessel endothelial cells causing vasculitis. Infections like subacute bacterial endocarditis, Epstein-Barr virus, and HIV may present with cutaneous leukocytoclastic vasculitis and occasional systemic involvement.
keyPoints: Rickettsias can cause vasculitis by invading endothelial cells of small blood vessels.
Inflammatory responses in systemic fungal diseases like histoplasmosis can mimic primary vasculitis.
Subacute bacterial endocarditis, Epstein-Barr virus infection, and HIV can present with cutaneous leukocytoclastic vasculitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES

items.subtopic: VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES
items.title: Connective Tissue Diseases and Secondary Vasculitis: Clinical Features and Associations
items.content: Several connective tissue diseases exhibit vasculitis as a secondary manifestation. Systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory myositis, relapsing polychondritis, and Sjögren's syndrome are primary examples where small-vessel venulitis is common, often isolated to the skin but can occasionally lead to systemic necrotizing vasculitis. Other associations include ulcerative colitis, complement deficiencies, sarcoidosis, primary biliary cirrhosis, $ \alpha_{1} $-antitrypsin deficiency, and intestinal bypass surgery.
keyPoints: Vasculitis can be a secondary manifestation in connective tissue diseases like systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis.
Common form of vasculitis in these conditions is small-vessel venulitis isolated to the skin.
Systemic necrotizing vasculitis may occur in certain patients with these underlying diseases.
Secondary vasculitis associations include ulcerative colitis, complement deficiencies, sarcoidosis, primary biliary cirrhosis, $ \alpha_{1} $-antitrypsin deficiency, and intestinal bypass surgery.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES
