items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Polyarteritis nodosa commonly involves the pulmonary arteries.
items.explanation: Pulmonary arteries are not involved in polyarteritis nodosa.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Polyarteritis nodosa is a very uncommon disease.
items.explanation: As currently defined, polyarteritis nodosa is considered very uncommon.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: ANCA are commonly found in patients with polyarteritis nodosa.
items.explanation: ANCA are rarely found in patients with polyarteritis nodosa.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Renal involvement in polyarteritis nodosa can manifest as hypertension, renal insufficiency, or hemorrhage.
items.explanation: These are common manifestations of renal involvement in polyarteritis nodosa.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Polyarteritis Nodosa | DEFINITION
items.statement: Death in untreated polyarteritis nodosa usually results from cardiovascular causes.
items.explanation: Death usually results from gastrointestinal complications and cardiovascular causes.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Polyarteritis Nodosa | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: Polyarteritis Nodosa | DEFINITION
items.statement: Giant cell arteritis is a systemic disease that can involve arteries in multiple locations.
items.explanation: Giant cell arteritis characteristically involves one or more branches of the carotid artery but is a systemic disease that can affect multiple locations.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Polyarteritis Nodosa | DEFINITION

items.subtopic: INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Giant cell arteritis has a higher incidence in southern Europe compared to Scandinavia.
items.explanation: A high incidence of giant cell arteritis is found in Scandinavia, not southern Europe.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Giant cell arteritis involves only the temporal artery.
items.explanation: Patients often have a systemic vasculitis of multiple medium- and large-sized arteries, not just the temporal artery.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Most patients with giant cell arteritis present with complaints relating to the head or eyes before experiencing visual loss.
items.explanation: Patients typically have symptoms related to the head or eyes prior to any visual loss.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Large-vessel disease is always the primary presentation of giant cell arteritis.
items.explanation: Large-vessel disease can be a primary or secondary manifestation, not always primary.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Therapy for giant cell arteritis should be delayed until diagnostic studies are completed.
items.explanation: Therapy should not be delayed pending the performance of diagnostic studies in cases suggestive of giant cell arteritis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Serum creatine kinase levels are elevated in polymyalgia rheumatica.
items.explanation: Levels of enzymes indicative of muscle damage such as serum creatine kinase are not elevated in isolated polymyalgia rheumatica.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Giant Cell Arteritis
items.statement: Patients with giant cell arteritis are at increased risk of developing thoracic aortic aneurysms.
items.explanation: Patients with giant cell arteritis are 18 times more likely to develop thoracic aortic aneurysms than the general population.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Giant Cell Arteritis

items.subtopic: Giant Cell Arteritis
items.statement: Infliximab has been proven effective in treating giant cell arteritis.
items.explanation: Infliximab was studied and not found to provide benefit for giant cell arteritis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Giant Cell Arteritis

items.subtopic: Polymyalgia Rheumatica | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Infliximab was found to lessen relapse or glucocorticoid requirements in polymyalgia rheumatica.
items.explanation: A randomized trial did not find infliximab to lessen relapse or glucocorticoid requirements.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Polymyalgia Rheumatica | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Polymyalgia Rheumatica | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Takayasu arteritis is characterized by a strong predilection for the aortic arch and its branches.
items.explanation: The disease involves large-sized arteries with a strong predilection for the aortic arch and its branches.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Polymyalgia Rheumatica | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis
items.statement: Takayasu arteritis is most often chronic and relapsing.
items.explanation: The course of the disease is variable, and although spontaneous remissions may occur, Takayasu arteritis is most often chronic and relapsing.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis
items.statement: Tocilizumab has been proven to be effective as a primary treatment for Takayasu arteritis in randomized trials.
items.explanation: Tocilizumab did not reach its primary efficacy endpoint in a randomized trial, although it was associated with glucocorticoid-sparing effects and improvements in well-being.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Renal involvement in IgA vasculitis typically requires aggressive therapy.
items.explanation: Renal involvement usually resolves spontaneously without therapy.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: IgA vasculitis is more common in adults than children.
items.explanation: IgA vasculitis is usually seen in children ages 4–7 years.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | IgA Vasculitis | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Renal biopsy is routinely required for the diagnosis of IgA vasculitis.
items.explanation: Renal biopsy is rarely needed for the diagnosis of IgA vasculitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS | IgA Vasculitis | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | IgA Vasculitis | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Mortality in IgA vasculitis is exceedingly rare.
items.explanation: The prognosis of IgA vasculitis is excellent with mortality being exceedingly rare.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS | IgA Vasculitis | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis
items.statement: Acute mortality directly from cryoglobulinemic vasculitis is common.
items.explanation: Acute mortality directly from cryoglobulinemic vasculitis is uncommon.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis
items.statement: Antiviral therapy should be considered first-line treatment for hepatitis C-associated cryoglobulinemic vasculitis.
items.explanation: Treatment with antiviral therapy is first-line therapy for hepatitis C-associated cryoglobulinemic vasculitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis

items.subtopic: SINGLE-ORGAN VASCULITIS | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Cutaneous vasculitis is a specific disease entity.
items.explanation: Cutaneous vasculitis is not one specific disease but a manifestation that can be seen in various settings.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · SINGLE-ORGAN VASCULITIS | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: SINGLE-ORGAN VASCULITIS | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: The exact incidence of idiopathic cutaneous vasculitis is well-determined.
items.explanation: The exact incidence of idiopathic cutaneous vasculitis has not been determined.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · SINGLE-ORGAN VASCULITIS | DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis
items.statement: Idiopathic cutaneous vasculitis is generally limited to the skin and rarely leads to life-threatening situations.
items.explanation: The disease is generally limited to the skin, so lack of consistent response to therapy usually does not lead to a life-threatening situation.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis
items.statement: Cyclophosphamide is commonly used for treating idiopathic cutaneous vasculitis.
items.explanation: Cyclophosphamide should almost never be used for idiopathic cutaneous vasculitis because of the potential toxicity.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Idiopathic Cutaneous Vasculitis

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM VASCULITIS | BEHCET'S DISEASE | COGAN'S SYNDROME
items.statement: Systemic symptoms are commonly present in primary CNS vasculitis.
items.explanation: Systemic symptoms are generally absent in primary CNS vasculitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM VASCULITIS | BEHCET'S DISEASE | COGAN'S SYNDROME

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM VASCULITIS | BEHCET'S DISEASE | COGAN'S SYNDROME
items.statement: Glucocorticoid therapy can induce clinical remissions in granulomatous primary CNS vasculitis.
items.explanation: Reports indicate glucocorticoid therapy, alone or with cyclophosphamide, has induced clinical remissions.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · PRIMARY CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM VASCULITIS | BEHCET'S DISEASE | COGAN'S SYNDROME

items.subtopic: KAWASAKI'S DISEASE
items.statement: The prognosis for Kawasaki's disease is generally poor.
items.explanation: Apart from a small percentage of fatal complications, the prognosis for uneventful recovery in Kawasaki's disease is excellent.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · KAWASAKI'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: KAWASAKI'S DISEASE
items.statement: Multisystem inflammatory syndrome (MIS-C) can resemble Kawasaki's disease.
items.explanation: MIS-C, observed with SARS-CoV-2 infections, may present clinical features similar to those of Kawasaki's disease.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · KAWASAKI'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: POLYANGIITIS OVERLAP SYNDROMES | DRUG-INDUCED VASCULITIS | SERUM SICKNESS AND SERUM SICKNESS-LIKE REACTIONS
items.statement: Serum sickness-like reactions are always due to vasculitis.
items.explanation: Most manifestations of serum sickness-like reactions are not due to vasculitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · POLYANGIITIS OVERLAP SYNDROMES | DRUG-INDUCED VASCULITIS | SERUM SICKNESS AND SERUM SICKNESS-LIKE REACTIONS

items.subtopic: POLYANGIITIS OVERLAP SYNDROMES | DRUG-INDUCED VASCULITIS | SERUM SICKNESS AND SERUM SICKNESS-LIKE REACTIONS
items.statement: Patients with life-threatening small-vessel vasculitis should be treated with glucocorticoids and cyclophosphamide.
items.explanation: Such patients are initially treated with glucocorticoids and cyclophosphamide as described for granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · POLYANGIITIS OVERLAP SYNDROMES | DRUG-INDUCED VASCULITIS | SERUM SICKNESS AND SERUM SICKNESS-LIKE REACTIONS

items.subtopic: VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES
items.statement: Subacute bacterial endocarditis can be associated with cutaneous leukocytoclastic vasculitis.
items.explanation: Cutaneous leukocytoclastic vasculitis may be a component of some infections, including subacute bacterial endocarditis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES

items.subtopic: VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES
items.statement: Systemic necrotizing vasculitis is the most common form of vasculitis in connective tissue diseases.
items.explanation: The most common form of vasculitis in these conditions is small-vessel venulitis isolated to the skin.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES

items.subtopic: The Vasculitis Syndromes | DEFINITION | CLASSIFICATION | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | PATHOGENIC IMMUNE-COMPLEX FORMATION
items.assertion: Vasculitis can be a primary manifestation of a disease.
items.reason: Vasculitis may also be a secondary component of another disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why vasculitis can be a primary manifestation.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · The Vasculitis Syndromes | DEFINITION | CLASSIFICATION | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | PATHOGENIC IMMUNE-COMPLEX FORMATION

items.subtopic: The Vasculitis Syndromes | DEFINITION | CLASSIFICATION | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | PATHOGENIC IMMUNE-COMPLEX FORMATION
items.assertion: All patients with active vasculitis have demonstrable circulating or deposited immune complexes.
items.reason: Immune complex formation is the first and most widely accepted pathogenic mechanism of vasculitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because many patients with active vasculitis do not have demonstrable circulating or deposited immune complexes.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · The Vasculitis Syndromes | DEFINITION | CLASSIFICATION | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | PATHOGENIC IMMUNE-COMPLEX FORMATION

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)
items.assertion: Methotrexate is contraindicated in renal insufficiency.
items.reason: Methotrexate is eliminated by the kidney and its use increases the risk for toxicity in renal insufficiency.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why methotrexate is contraindicated in renal insufficiency.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)
items.assertion: Cyclophosphamide should be taken with a large amount of fluid throughout the day.
items.reason: This practice reduces the risk of bladder injury and requires monitoring for bladder cancer indefinitely.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why cyclophosphamide should be taken with a large amount of fluid.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF TREATMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.assertion: Cocaine-induced tissue injury can mimic granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
items.reason: ANCA targeting human neutrophil elastase can be found in patients with cocaine-induced midline destructive lesions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.assertion: Levamisole adulteration of cocaine can result in cutaneous infarction.
items.reason: This condition is associated with granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; levamisole-induced disease is not associated with granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.assertion: Active glomerulonephritis in granulomatosis with polyangiitis is always considered severe.
items.reason: Even when renal function remains normal, active glomerulonephritis is classified as severe disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why active glomerulonephritis is always considered severe.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: REMISSION INDUCTION OF SEVERE DISEASE
items.assertion: Avacopan can be used as an alternative to glucocorticoids in patients receiving induction with cyclophosphamide or rituximab.
items.reason: A randomized trial showed higher sustained remission and similar rates of serious adverse events for avacopan compared to prednisone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · REMISSION INDUCTION OF SEVERE DISEASE

items.subtopic: INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Microscopic Polyangiitis
items.assertion: The renal lesion in microscopic polyangiitis is histologically identical to that of granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
items.reason: Both conditions are highly associated with ANCA, which may play a role in their pathogenesis.
items.explanation: While both the assertion and reason are true, the similarity in renal lesions is not explained by their association with ANCA.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Microscopic Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
items.assertion: The prognosis of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis is poor without treatment.
items.reason: Untreated, the 5-year survival rate is only 25%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS | Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS
items.assertion: Polyarteritis nodosa can be diagnosed with the presence of aneurysms alone.
items.reason: Aneurysms are pathognomonic for polyarteritis nodosa.
items.explanation: Aneurysms of vessels are not pathognomonic of polyarteritis nodosa; they may also be absent or limited to stenotic segments.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.assertion: A trial of glucocorticoid therapy can support the diagnosis of giant cell arteritis.
items.reason: Temporal artery biopsies may show vasculitis even after ~14 days of glucocorticoid therapy.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why a trial of glucocorticoid therapy supports the diagnosis; rather, it suggests that biopsy results might be affected by prior treatment.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Giant Cell Arteritis
items.assertion: The use of aspirin is recommended in patients with giant cell arteritis.
items.reason: Aspirin has been found to reduce the occurrence of cranial ischemic complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why aspirin use is recommended.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · Giant Cell Arteritis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis
items.assertion: The diagnosis of Takayasu arteritis can be confirmed by characteristic patterns on arteriography.
items.reason: Arteriography shows irregular vessel walls, stenosis, poststenotic dilation, aneurysm formation, occlusion, and increased collateral circulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis
items.assertion: Methotrexate is used in doses up to 25 mg per week for patients with Takayasu arteritis who are refractory to glucocorticoids.
items.reason: Retrospective series have shown benefit from methotrexate use in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Takayasu Arteritis

items.subtopic: DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS
items.assertion: IgA vasculitis is characterized by immune-complex deposition.
items.reason: The pathogenic mechanism for IgA vasculitis involves the deposition of immune complexes, with IgA being the most common antibody class in these complexes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · DEFINITION | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE | PATHOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis
items.assertion: Patients with cryoglobulinemic vasculitis who clear hepatitis C from the blood show improvement in their vasculitis.
items.reason: Clearing hepatitis C leads to reductions in levels of circulating cryoglobulins, IgM, and rheumatoid factor.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the improvement.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS | Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis

items.subtopic: VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES
items.assertion: Inflammatory myositis can have vasculitis as a secondary manifestation.
items.reason: Connective tissue diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis have vasculitis as a secondary manifestation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why inflammatory myositis can have vasculitis as a secondary manifestation.
items.reference: The Vasculitis Syndromes · VASCULITIS ASSOCIATED WITH OTHER UNDERLYING DISEASES
